verses.commentary: The first covenant had regulations for worship and an earthly sanctuary. The Mosaic system was elaborate and God-given but earthly and temporary. It functioned according to divine prescription but pointed beyond itself.
A tabernacle was set up. The first room (Holy Place) contained the lampstand and table with consecrated bread. This was the outer sanctuary where priests ministered daily.

Behind the second curtain was the Most Holy Place. The veil separated the Holy Place from the holiest sanctuary. Only the high priest could enter, and only once a year. Access was severely restricted.

The Most Holy Place contained the golden altar of incense and the gold-covered ark of the covenant. Inside the ark: manna, Aaron's staff, and the stone tablets. These items symbolized God's provision, authority, and law.

Above the ark were the cherubim of glory, overshadowing the atonement cover (mercy seat). But we cannot discuss these things in detail now. The writer acknowledges the richness of symbolism but presses on to the main point: Christ's superior sacrifice.
Priests entered regularly into the outer room to carry on their ministry. Daily, routine service characterized the Holy Place. Repetition was necessary because cleansing was never final.

But only the high priest entered the inner room, once a year, and never without blood, which he offered for himself and for the sins the people had committed in ignorance. Limited access, repeated sacrifice, and incomplete atonement characterized the old system.

The Holy Spirit was showing that the way into the Most Holy Place had not yet been disclosed as long as the first tabernacle was still functioning. The restricted access was pedagogical. God was teaching that sin blocked the way to His presence.

This is an illustration for the present time, indicating that the gifts and sacrifices being offered were not able to clear the conscience of the worshiper. Sacrifices cleansed ceremonially but not conscientiously. They dealt with external defilement, not internal guilt.

They are only a matter of food and drink and various ceremonial washings—external regulations applying until the time of the new order. The old system regulated outward actions until Christ brought inward transformation.

But when Christ came as high priest of the good things now already here, He went through the greater and more perfect tabernacle not made with human hands. Christ's ministry takes place in the heavenly sanctuary, the true reality of which the earthly was only a shadow.

He did not enter by means of the blood of goats and calves but by His own blood, thus obtaining eternal redemption. Animal blood brought temporary, external cleansing. Christ's blood secured eternal, internal redemption. Once for all replaces year after year.

The blood of goats and bulls and the ashes of a heifer sprinkled on the ceremonially unclean sanctified them so they were outwardly clean. Old covenant sacrifices achieved ceremonial purity—external, temporary, symbolic.

How much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered Himself unblemished to God, cleanse our consciences from acts that lead to death, so that we may serve the living God! Christ's blood cleanses internally and eternally. Conscience is purged. Service to God becomes possible.

For this reason Christ is the mediator of a new covenant, that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance—now that He has died as a ransom to set them free from sins committed under the first covenant. Christ's death validates both covenants. It releases Old Testament saints and secures New Testament believers.

In the case of a will (diathēkē), it is necessary to prove the death of the one who made it. The Greek word diathēkē means both covenant and will. Here the testamentary sense is in view. A will is ineffective until the testator dies.
Because a will is in force only when somebody has died; it never takes effect while the one who made it is living. Death activates a will. Christ's death put the new covenant into effect.
This is why even the first covenant was not put into effect without blood. Blood ratification was essential even for the Mosaic covenant. Exodus 24:8 is in view. Blood seals covenants.

When Moses proclaimed every command of the law, he took the blood of calves, with water, scarlet wool and hyssop, and sprinkled the scroll and all the people. The covenant ratification ceremony involved blood applied to both the word (scroll) and the people.

He said, This is the blood of the covenant, which God has commanded you to keep. Exodus 24:8 quoted. Jesus echoed this at the Last Supper: This is My blood of the new covenant. Blood establishes covenant relationship.
He sprinkled with blood both the tabernacle and everything used in its ceremonies. Comprehensive consecration through blood. Everything connected with worship required blood purification.

In fact, the law requires that nearly everything be cleansed with blood, and without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness. This is the foundational principle of atonement. Blood represents life given in death. Forgiveness requires a life laid down.

It was necessary for the copies of the heavenly things to be purified with these sacrifices, but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices than these. Earthly shadows required animal blood. Heavenly realities required Christ's blood. The true sanctuary demanded a superior sacrifice.

For Christ did not enter a sanctuary made with human hands that was only a copy of the true one; He entered heaven itself, now to appear for us in God's presence. Christ entered the true Holy of Holies—heaven. He appears before God on our behalf as our representative and advocate.

Nor did He enter heaven to offer Himself again and again, the way the high priest enters the Most Holy Place every year with blood that is not his own. Repetition characterized the old system. Christ's single offering renders repetition unnecessary and impossible.

Otherwise Christ would have had to suffer many times since the creation of the world. But He has appeared once for all at the culmination of the ages to do away with sin by the sacrifice of Himself. Once for all (ephapax) is emphatic. Christ's sacrifice is unrepeatable because it is sufficient. The culmination of the ages—all history pointed to this moment.

Just as people are destined to die once, and after that to face judgment. Universal human pattern: one death, then judgment. No reincarnation. No second chances. Death is the boundary; judgment follows.

So Christ was sacrificed once to take away the sins of many; and He will appear a second time, not to bear sin, but to bring salvation to those who are waiting for Him. Christ's first coming dealt with sin through sacrifice. His second coming brings final salvation without dealing with sin again. The work is complete.
